---
title: "Exercise 1 (P2CRC Update)"
format: html
editor: source
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this exercise, you will be able to:

-   Understand the structure of a `SpatialExperiment` object.
-   Access and interpret `spatialCoords` and `imgData`.
-   Perform basic subsetting operations on `SpatialExperiment` objects.
-   Combine multiple `SpatialExperiment` objects.
-   Apply image transformations (rotation, mirroring) to spatial data.

## Libraries

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-1 }
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| output: false

# load libraries to automatically include in renv
library(downlit)
library(xml2)
library(GenomeInfoDb)
library(scrapper)
library(SingleR)
library(BayesSpace)
library(xgboost)
library(namer)
library(styler)
```

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-2 }
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| output: false

# Load necessary R libraries for spatial transcriptomics data analysis.
library(SpatialExperiment)
library(VisiumIO)
library(arrow)
library(qs2)
library(ggspavis)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)
library(arrow)
library(scuttle)
library(HDF5Array)
```

## Data for the course

We will work on a Visium HD (v4.0 segmented) and Xenium dataset from a human colon cancer study (P2CRC) [recently published](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41588-025-02193-3). 

For this exercise, we assume you have already run the [Data Preparation](day1-0_data_preparation.qmd) steps. We will load the pre-subsetted objects that represent the same tissue region for both platforms.

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-3}
# Load the pre-prepared P2CRC objects
if (file.exists("../data/spe_hd_P2CRC_sub.rds")) {
  spe <- readRDS("../data/spe_hd_P2CRC_sub.rds")
} else {
  message("P2CRC data not found! Please run day1-0_data_preparation.qmd first.")
}
```

# The 'spe' object is now loaded from the pre-prepared RDS file in the previous step.
# It already contains the subsetted region of interest for P2CRC.

::: callout-important
## Exercise 1b

**What other bin sizes are available? Which is the smallest capture unit and what do the other bin sizes mean? Why wouldn't you always want to work with the smallest available bin?**

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

In the Visium HD output folder, you will find several binned outputs:

```         
square_002um
square_008um
square_016um
```

The capture bins are 2µm^2 (adjacent) on the VisiumHD slide. Larger bins are pools of 2 µm bins. Working with those could be handy because the number of molecules captured will be higher, reducing the sparsity of the data at the cost of resolution.
:::

## Exploring the object

In this first exercise, we will dive into the `SpatialExperiment` object, a cornerstone of spatial transcriptomics analysis in `R`. We will explore the different components of the loaded object, and perform basic manipulations. By the end of this session, you will have a solid understanding of how to handle spatial transcriptomics data within the `R`/`Bioconductor` ecosystem.

![Overview of the `SpatialExperiment` class structure](../assets/images/spe.png)

The different slots of the `SpatialExperiment` object can be approached with several accessor functions that correspond to their names. As the `SpatialExperiment` is an extension of the `SingleCellExperiment` class, methods developed to be applied on `SingleCellExperiment` objects (or by extension on `SummarizedExperiment` objects) can also be applied.

::: callout-important
## Exercise 2

Check out the outputs of the following functions, that you should already be familiar with:

-   `colData()`
-   `rowData()`
-   `assay()`
-   `reducedDims()`

Check out the outputs of the following functions, which are specific to the `SpatialExperiment` class:

-   `spatialCoords()`
-   `imgData()`
-   `imgRaster()`

Questions:

-   What kind of data is in the different slots?
-   How many spots and genes do we have retained in the `spe` object?
-   Are all spots within the tissue area?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

The slot `colData` contains metadata of each spot. You can check the dimensions using:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-5 }
colData(spe) |> head()
ncol(spe)
```

We have a column `in_tissue` here, and we can check whether spots are covered by the tissue:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-6 }
colData(spe) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  group_by(in_tissue) |>
  summarise(number = n())
```
The slot `rowData` contains metadata about the genes:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-7 }
rowData(spe) |> head()
nrow(spe)
```

The assay is the core count matrix of the object:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-8 }
assay(spe)
```
The slot for `reducedDims` is empty for now:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-9 }
reducedDims(spe)
```

The slot `spatialCoords` maps each spot to the full resolution image:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-10 }
spatialCoords(spe) |> head()
```
The slot `imgData` lists all available images linked to the dataset.

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-11 }
imgData(spe)
```

The H&E image can be exported as a raster image for plotting:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-12 }
imgRaster(spe) |> plot()
```

:::

We saw that the rownames of the object were Ensembl IDs. However, it's more convenient to have gene symbols. Therefore, we replace the Ensembl IDs, with the symbols stored in `rowData`:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-13 }
rownames(spe) <- uniquifyFeatureNames(ID = rowData(spe)$ID, names = rowData(spe)$Symbol)
```

To get a visual overview the slide we can use `ggspavis::plotVisium`. First without spots:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-14 }
plotVisium(spe, spots = FALSE)
```

And with spots overlayed:

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-15 }
plotVisium(spe, point_shape = 22, point_size = 0.5)
```

::: callout-important
## Exercise 3

Why do we see only spots in a specific area?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer
The `spe` object we loaded was already subsetted to a specific region of interest for P2CRC to allow for faster processing and side-by-side comparison with other platforms.

:::

It's possible to colour spots according to some variable, e.g., the number of read counts for a selected gene (i.e., PIGR): 

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-16 }
plotVisium(spe,
  annotate = "PIGR",
  assay = "counts",
  zoom = TRUE,
  point_size = 1,
  point_shape = 22
)
```
::: callout-important
## Exercise 4

Check out the usage of `plotVisium` with `?plotVisium`. Create a plot that zooms into the spots, and colors the spots according to column `array_row`.

In which slot is `array_row` stored and what does it represent?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

The column `array_row` is stored in `colData`.

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-17 }
p <- plotVisium(spe, annotate = "array_row", zoom = TRUE, point_shape = 22)
p
```
:::

## Retain only observations that map to tissue 

::: callout-important
## Exercise 5

Subset the SpatialExperiment object (and save it into a new object) to retain only spots that are within the tissue area

How many spots were present in "spe" object? And how many are left after selecting for in-tissue spots only (object sub)?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-18 }
# Subset to in-tissue spots only
sub <- spe[, colData(spe)$in_tissue]
```

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-19 }
# Check dimensions of original and subsetted objects
dim(spe)
dim(sub)
```
:::


## Save the object

Save the filtered `SpatialExperiment` object for the next steps:
```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-20 }
dir.create("results/day1", showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
saveHDF5SummarizedExperiment(spe,
  dir = "results/day1", prefix = "01.1_spe_p2", replace = TRUE,
  chunkdim = NULL, level = NULL, as.sparse = NA,
  verbose = NA
)
```

Clear your environment

```{r day1-1b-SpatialExperiment-21 }
#| eval: false
rm(list = ls())
gc()
.rs.restartR()
```

::: callout-important
**Key Takeaways:**

-   The `SpatialExperiment` class is a versatile container for spatial transcriptomics data.
-   It allows for easy access and manipulation of spatial coordinates, image data, and assay data.
-   Various operations like subsetting, combining, and image transformations can be performed efficiently.
:::
